{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | rank = | parents = Father: [[Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[Empress Inoe]] and [[Empress Kōken]].

Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>

In [[782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | rank = | parents = Father: [[Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[Empress Inoe]] and [[Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Volumes II & III), [[Kodansha Academic Library]], Translated by [[Takeshi Ujitani]], 1992-1995. * ''Nihon Kōki'' (Volume I), Kodansha Academic Library, Translated by [[Yasushi Morita]], 2006.
== Related Articles == * [[Prince Shioyaki]] * [[Empress Kōken]] * [[Inoe Naishinnō]] * [[Inba Nihon Medical University Station]]

This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Volumes II & III), [[Kodansha Academic Library]], Translated by [[Takeshi Ujitani]], 1992-1995. * ''Nihon Kōki'' (Volume I), Kodansha Academic Library, Translated by [[Yasushi Morita]], 2006.
== Related Articles == * [[Prince Shioyaki]] * [[Empress Kōken]] * [[Inoe Naishinnō]] * [[Inba Nihon Medical University Station]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | rank = | parents = Father: [[Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[Empress Inoe]] and [[Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 皇親 | 名 = 不破内親王 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[奈良時代]]-[[平安時代]]初期 | 生誕 = 不詳 | 死没 = 不詳 | 改名 = 不破（内親王）→（厨真人）厨女→不破（内親王） | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 尊号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 父母 = 父：[[聖武天皇]]、母：[[県犬養広刀自]] | 兄弟 = [[井上内親王]]、[[孝謙天皇]]、'''不破内親王'''、[[基王]]、[[安積親王]] | 夫 =[[塩焼王]]（氷上塩焼） | 子 = [[氷上志計志麻呂]]、[[氷上川継]]、娘（名不詳）数人 | 特記事項 = }} '''不破内親王'''（ふわないしんのう）は、[[聖武天皇]]の[[皇女]]。母は[[夫人]]・[[県犬養広刀自]]。[[光仁天皇]]の[[皇后]]・[[井上内親王]]の同母妹。 == 略歴 == 時期は不明であるが、[[新田部親王]]の子で[[天武天皇]]の孫にあたる[[塩焼王]]と[[結婚]]している。また、一時、[[内親王]]の身位を剥奪されたことがあったというが、具体的な時期や事情はわかっていない。河内祥輔はこれを父の聖武天皇の存命中の出来事{{Efn|『続日本紀』巻第二十九、神護景雲3年5月25日（壬辰）条には、「不破内親王者。先朝有勅。削親王名。」とあるが、孝謙上皇/称徳天皇の手で廃位された淳仁天皇を考慮しないとすれば、聖武天皇の時代の話となる。}}として父娘関係が良好でなかったとし、これが同母姉である井上内親王及びその夫・白壁王との待遇差を生んだとしている{{Cite book|和書|author=河内祥輔|title=古代政治史における天皇制の論理|edition=増訂版|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|origyear=1986|pages=136-137}}。 [[天平宝字]]8年9月18日（[[764年]]）、塩焼王が[[藤原仲麻呂の乱]]に参加して殺害されている『続日本紀』巻第二十五、廃帝　[[淳仁天皇]]、天平宝字8年9月29日条が、不破内親王と息子・[[氷上志計志麻呂]]は連坐を免れている。 [[神護景雲]]3年（[[769年]]）5月、[[県犬養姉女]]、新田部親王の娘である[[忍坂女王]]、[[石田女王]]と共謀して[[孝謙天皇|称徳天皇]]を呪詛し、志計志麻呂を皇位につけようとしたとして、再び内親王の身位を廃され、'''厨真人厨女'''（くりやのまひとくりやめ）と改名させられた上、[[平城京]]内の居住を禁じられた。志計志麻呂は[[土佐国]]に配流されている『続日本紀』巻第二十九、称徳天皇、神護景雲3年5月25日条。同年7月には、生活の資として、封戸40戸と水田10町を与えられている『続日本紀』巻第三十、称徳天皇、神護景雲3年7月10日条。 [[宝亀]]3年（[[772年]]）12月、呪詛事件は誣告による[[冤罪]]であったとして、内親王に復帰している『続日本紀』巻第三十二、[[光仁天皇]]、宝亀3年12月12日条。 [[延暦]]元年（[[782年]]）閏正月、息子の[[氷上川継]]{{Efn|氷上志計志麻呂と氷上川継を同一人物とする[[林陸朗]]の説がある林陸朗, 「[https://doi.org/10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1 桓武朝初世の政治情勢]」『國學院大學北海道短期大学部紀要』30号, 國學院大學北海道短期大学部, 2013年,p10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}。}}が謀反を起こそうとしたとして[[伊豆国]]に配流されたのに連坐して、娘たちとともに[[淡路国]]に配流された『続日本紀』巻第三十七、[[桓武天皇]]　今皇帝　天応2年閏1月11日条、閏1月14日条。延暦14年（[[795年]]）12月、[[和泉国]]に移された『日本後紀』巻第四、桓武天皇、延暦14年12月22日条（逸文）。以後の消息は不明である。 == 伝承 == [[ファイル:松虫姫公園.jpg|サムネイル|250x250ピクセル|松虫姫公園にある、松虫姫と池に身を投じたという牛のオブジェ]] [[千葉県]][[印西市]]に所在する[[松虫寺]]に、不破内親王にまつわる伝承が残る。 聖武天皇の第三皇女不破内親王が[[ハンセン病]]に罹り、内裏から追放された{{Cite|和書|ref=harv|title=佐倉文庫第二集 佐倉風土記|author=佐倉市教育委員会編|date=1974-03|publisher=佐倉市教育委員会|pages=53}}{{Cite|和書|ref=harv|title=[[利根川図志]]|author=[[赤松宗旦]]著・[[柳田国男]]校訂|date=1938-11|publisher=[[岩波書店]]|pages=209}}。内親王が薬師如来に祈ったところ、病が快癒した。寺の後ろの墳に松虫姫が祀られているという。その後、内親王は都に戻って薨去しその遺骨が安置されたとする伝承や、天皇が薬師仏のために堂宇を建てて松虫寺としたという伝承{{Cite web |title=デジタルミュージアム『日本博覧図』～摩尼珠山松虫寺境内全図 |url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html |website=www.chiba-muse.or.jp |accessdate=2022-03-06 |publisher=千葉県立中央博物館}}も残る。 [[大衆文学研究会]]編『房総の不思議な話、珍しい話』（[[崙書房]]、1983年）によれば{{要ページ番号|section=1|date=2022-03}}、次のような内容である。
不破内親王は幼名を'''松虫姫'''といったが、不幸にして[[ハンセン病|癩]]を患い、手の施しようがなかったが、夢に、[[下総国]]に効験あらたかな薬師如来が鎮座するとの託宣があった。姫がこれを信じて東国に下ったところ、下総国印旛郡の萩原郷にはたして夢に見た薬師堂があり、かの地に庵を結んで一心に平癒を祈り、ついに全快することを得た。聖武天皇はこれを喜び、[[行基]]に命じて一寺を建立させ「[[松虫寺]]」と称したという。
現在も松虫寺には「松虫姫御廟」と呼ばれる堂がある{{Cite web |title=松虫寺 旅行 観光 旅 国内13万件の全国観光情報検索サイト 全国観るなび 全国 |url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/ |website=全国観光情報サイト 全国観るなび（日本観光振興協会） |accessdate=2022-03-06 |language=ja}}。また付近の千葉ニュータウン印旛日本医大地区内にある松虫姫公園には、姫と一緒に京から来て、姫が帰るにあたっては、年老いていたためこの地に残されることとなり、姫との別れを悲しんで池に身を投じたという牛を模したオブジェがある。 [[江戸時代]]、[[佐倉惣五郎]]を主人公とする[[実録本]]において、不破内親王の伝承が取り入れられ、佐倉惣五郎の先祖を不破内親王と共に下向した武士の末裔とする物語が広まった{{Cite journal|和書|author=荻原大地|date=2018-09|title=<場>の虚構・<血縁者>の虚構 -『佐倉花実物語』の創作方法-|url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/00057698|journal=早稲田大学大学院教育学研究科紀要 : 別冊|volume=26|issue=1|pages=15-24|publisher=早稲田大学教育学研究科|ref=harv}}。 == 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『[[続日本紀]]』全現代語訳（中）(下）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1992年・1995年 * 『[[日本後紀]]』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、[[森田悌]]：訳、2006年 == 関連項目 == * [[塩焼王]] * [[孝謙天皇]] * [[井上内親王]] * [[印旛日本医大駅]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{日本の内親王一覧}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ふわないしんのう}} [[Category:8世紀日本の女性]] [[Category:日本の内親王]] [[Category:日本の皇族の妃]] [[category:奈良時代の女性皇族]] [[Category:聖武天皇の子女]] [[Category:皇籍に復帰した人物]]

{{基礎情報 皇親 | 名 = 不破内親王 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[奈良時代]]-[[平安時代]]初期 | 生誕 = 不詳 | 死没 = 不詳 | 改名 = 不破（内親王）→（厨真人）厨女→不破（内親王） | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 尊号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 父母 = 父：[[聖武天皇]]、母：[[県犬養広刀自]] | 兄弟 = [[井上内親王]]、[[孝謙天皇]]、'''不破内親王'''、[[基王]]、[[安積親王]] | 夫 =[[塩焼王]]（氷上塩焼） | 子 = [[氷上志計志麻呂]]、[[氷上川継]]、娘（名不詳）数人 | 特記事項 = }} '''不破内親王'''（ふわないしんのう）は、[[聖武天皇]]の[[皇女]]。母は[[夫人]]・[[県犬養広刀自]]。[[光仁天皇]]の[[皇后]]・[[井上内親王]]の同母妹。 == 略歴 == 時期は不明であるが、[[新田部親王]]の子で[[天武天皇]]の孫にあたる[[塩焼王]]と[[結婚]]している。また、一時、[[内親王]]の身位を剥奪されたことがあったというが、具体的な時期や事情はわかっていない。河内祥輔はこれを父の聖武天皇の存命中の出来事{{Efn|『続日本紀』巻第二十九、神護景雲3年5月25日（壬辰）条には、「不破内親王者。先朝有勅。削親王名。」とあるが、孝謙上皇/称徳天皇の手で廃位された淳仁天皇を考慮しないとすれば、聖武天皇の時代の話となる。}}として父娘関係が良好でなかったとし、これが同母姉である井上内親王及びその夫・白壁王との待遇差を生んだとしている{{Cite book|和書|author=河内祥輔|title=古代政治史における天皇制の論理|edition=増訂版|publisher=吉川弘文館|year=2014|origyear=1986|pages=136-137}}。 [[天平宝字]]8年9月18日（[[764年]]）、塩焼王が[[藤原仲麻呂の乱]]に参加して殺害されている『続日本紀』巻第二十五、廃帝　[[淳仁天皇]]、天平宝字8年9月29日条が、不破内親王と息子・[[氷上志計志麻呂]]は連坐を免れている。 [[神護景雲]]3年（[[769年]]）5月、[[県犬養姉女]]、新田部親王の娘である[[忍坂女王]]、[[石田女王]]と共謀して[[孝謙天皇|称徳天皇]]を呪詛し、志計志麻呂を皇位につけようとしたとして、再び内親王の身位を廃され、'''厨真人厨女'''（くりやのまひとくりやめ）と改名させられた上、[[平城京]]内の居住を禁じられた。志計志麻呂は[[土佐国]]に配流されている『続日本紀』巻第二十九、称徳天皇、神護景雲3年5月25日条。同年7月には、生活の資として、封戸40戸と水田10町を与えられている『続日本紀』巻第三十、称徳天皇、神護景雲3年7月10日条。 [[宝亀]]3年（[[772年]]）12月、呪詛事件は誣告による[[冤罪]]であったとして、内親王に復帰している『続日本紀』巻第三十二、[[光仁天皇]]、宝亀3年12月12日条。 [[延暦]]元年（[[782年]]）閏正月、息子の[[氷上川継]]{{Efn|氷上志計志麻呂と氷上川継を同一人物とする[[林陸朗]]の説がある林陸朗, 「[https://doi.org/10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1 桓武朝初世の政治情勢]」『國學院大學北海道短期大学部紀要』30号, 國學院大學北海道短期大学部, 2013年,p10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}。}}が謀反を起こそうとしたとして[[伊豆国]]に配流されたのに連坐して、娘たちとともに[[淡路国]]に配流された『続日本紀』巻第三十七、[[桓武天皇]]　今皇帝　天応2年閏1月11日条、閏1月14日条。延暦14年（[[795年]]）12月、[[和泉国]]に移された『日本後紀』巻第四、桓武天皇、延暦14年12月22日条（逸文）。以後の消息は不明である。 == 伝承 == [[ファイル:松虫姫公園.jpg|サムネイル|250x250ピクセル|松虫姫公園にある、松虫姫と池に身を投じたという牛のオブジェ]] [[千葉県]][[印西市]]に所在する[[松虫寺]]に、不破内親王にまつわる伝承が残る。 聖武天皇の第三皇女不破内親王が[[ハンセン病]]に罹り、内裏から追放された{{Cite|和書|ref=harv|title=佐倉文庫第二集 佐倉風土記|author=佐倉市教育委員会編|date=1974-03|publisher=佐倉市教育委員会|pages=53}}{{Cite|和書|ref=harv|title=[[利根川図志]]|author=[[赤松宗旦]]著・[[柳田国男]]校訂|date=1938-11|publisher=[[岩波書店]]|pages=209}}。内親王が薬師如来に祈ったところ、病が快癒した。寺の後ろの墳に松虫姫が祀られているという。その後、内親王は都に戻って薨去しその遺骨が安置されたとする伝承や、天皇が薬師仏のために堂宇を建てて松虫寺としたという伝承{{Cite web |title=デジタルミュージアム『日本博覧図』～摩尼珠山松虫寺境内全図 |url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html |website=www.chiba-muse.or.jp |accessdate=2022-03-06 |publisher=千葉県立中央博物館}}も残る。 [[大衆文学研究会]]編『房総の不思議な話、珍しい話』（[[崙書房]]、1983年）によれば{{要ページ番号|section=1|date=2022-03}}、次のような内容である。
不破内親王は幼名を'''松虫姫'''といったが、不幸にして[[ハンセン病|癩]]を患い、手の施しようがなかったが、夢に、[[下総国]]に効験あらたかな薬師如来が鎮座するとの託宣があった。姫がこれを信じて東国に下ったところ、下総国印旛郡の萩原郷にはたして夢に見た薬師堂があり、かの地に庵を結んで一心に平癒を祈り、ついに全快することを得た。聖武天皇はこれを喜び、[[行基]]に命じて一寺を建立させ「[[松虫寺]]」と称したという。
現在も松虫寺には「松虫姫御廟」と呼ばれる堂がある{{Cite web |title=松虫寺 旅行 観光 旅 国内13万件の全国観光情報検索サイト 全国観るなび 全国 |url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/ |website=全国観光情報サイト 全国観るなび（日本観光振興協会） |accessdate=2022-03-06 |language=ja}}。また付近の千葉ニュータウン印旛日本医大地区内にある松虫姫公園には、姫と一緒に京から来て、姫が帰るにあたっては、年老いていたためこの地に残されることとなり、姫との別れを悲しんで池に身を投じたという牛を模したオブジェがある。 [[江戸時代]]、[[佐倉惣五郎]]を主人公とする[[実録本]]において、不破内親王の伝承が取り入れられ、佐倉惣五郎の先祖を不破内親王と共に下向した武士の末裔とする物語が広まった{{Cite journal|和書|author=荻原大地|date=2018-09|title=<場>の虚構・<血縁者>の虚構 -『佐倉花実物語』の創作方法-|url=https://hdl.handle.net/2065/00057698|journal=早稲田大学大学院教育学研究科紀要 : 別冊|volume=26|issue=1|pages=15-24|publisher=早稲田大学教育学研究科|ref=harv}}。 == 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 『[[続日本紀]]』全現代語訳（中）(下）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1992年・1995年 * 『[[日本後紀]]』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、[[森田悌]]：訳、2006年 == 関連項目 == * [[塩焼王]] * [[孝謙天皇]] * [[井上内親王]] * [[印旛日本医大駅]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{日本の内親王一覧}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ふわないしんのう}} [[Category:8世紀日本の女性]] [[Category:日本の内親王]] [[Category:日本の皇族の妃]] [[category:奈良時代の女性皇族]] [[Category:聖武天皇の子女]] [[Category:皇籍に復帰した人物]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | parents = Father: [[Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[Empress Inoe]] and [[Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Volumes II & III), [[Kodansha Academic Library]], Translated by [[Takeshi Ujitani]], 1992-1995. * ''Nihon Kōki'' (Volume I), Kodansha Academic Library, Translated by [[Yasushi Morita]], 2006.
== Related Articles == * [[Prince Shioyaki]] * [[Empress Kōken]] * [[Inoe Naishinnō]] * [[Inba Nihon Medical University Station]]

{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | parents = Father: [[Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[Empress Inoe]] and [[Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Volumes II & III), [[Kodansha Academic Library]], Translated by [[Takeshi Ujitani]], 1992-1995. * ''Nihon Kōki'' (Volume I), Kodansha Academic Library, Translated by [[Yasushi Morita]], 2006.
== Related Articles == * [[Prince Shioyaki]] * [[Empress Kōken]] * [[Inoe Naishinnō]] * [[Inba Nihon Medical University Station]]

A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.

revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | parents = Father: [[Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[Empress Inoe]] and [[Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[:en:764|764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[:en:769|769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[:en:772|772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.

Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[:en:795|795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.

{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | parents = Father: [[Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[Empress Inoe]] and [[Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[:en:764|764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[:en:769|769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[:en:Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[:en:772|772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[:en:782|782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[:en:795|795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.

'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[Empress Inoe]] and [[Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[:en:764|764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[:en:769|769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[:en:Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[:en:772|772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[:en:782|782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[:en:Awaji Province|Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[:en:795|795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Volumes II & III), [[Kodansha Academic Library]], Translated by [[Takeshi Ujitani]], 1992-1995. * ''Nihon Kōki'' (Volume I), Kodansha Academic Library, Translated by [[Yasushi Morita]], 2006.
== Related Articles == * [[Prince Shioyaki]] * [[Empress Kōken]] * [[Inoe Naishinnō]] * [[Inba Nihon Medical University Station]]

The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.

{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | parents = Father: [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[:en:Empress Inoe|Empress Inoe]] and [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[:en:764|764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[:en:769|769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[:en:Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[:en:772|772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[:en:782|782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[:en:Awaji Province|Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[:en:795|795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.

{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | parents = Father: [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[:en:Empress Inoe|Empress Inoe]] and [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[:en:764|764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[:en:769|769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[:en:Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[:en:772|772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[:en:782|782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[:en:Izu Province|Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[:en:Awaji Province|Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[:en:795|795]], she was moved to [[Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Shoku Nihongi'' (Volumes II & III), [[Kodansha Academic Library]], Translated by [[Takeshi Ujitani]], 1992-1995. * ''Nihon Kōki'' (Volume I), Kodansha Academic Library, Translated by [[Yasushi Morita]], 2006.
== Related Articles == * [[Prince Shioyaki]] * [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] * [[Inoe Naishinnō]] * [[Inba Nihon Medical University Station]]

== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[:en:leprosy|leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.

revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | parents = Father: [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]], Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]], '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]]. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[:en:Empress Inoe|Empress Inoe]] and [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]].
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]]. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, [[:en:764|764]], Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In [[:en:769|769]], Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside [[:en:Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|Agata no Inukai no Anenoko]], [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to [[Tosa Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December [[:en:772|772]], the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In [[:en:782|782]], her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to [[:en:Izu Province|Izu Province]]. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to [[:en:Awaji Province|Awaji Province]].<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In [[:en:795|795]], she was moved to [[:en:Izumi Province|Izumi Province]].<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted [[:en:leprosy|leprosy]] and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in [[Shimōsa Province]]. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered [[:en:Gyōki|Gyōki]] to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.
