Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to {{ill|Awaji Province|en|Awaji Province|WD=Q1053717|lt=Awaji Province|1=Awaji Province}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In {{ill|795|en|795|WD=Q30447|lt=795|1=795}}, she was moved to {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}}.<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.

The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted {{ill|leprosy|en|leprosy|WD=Q36956|lt=leprosy|1=Leprosy}} and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.
== Footnotes == === Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:不破内親王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:不破内親王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:不破内親王]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=0723|death_date=0795|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Shioyaki-ō|WD=Q11428805|1=Shioyaki-ō}}|children={{ill|Higami no Kawatsugu|WD=Q11549549|1=Higami no Kawatsugu}}; {{ill|Q11549551|WD=Q11549551|1=Hikami no Shikeshimaro}}|father={{ill|Shōmu|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}}|mother={{ill|Agatainukai no Hirotoji|WD=Q11582687|1=Agatainukai no Hirotoji}}|relatives={{ill|Asaka-shinnō|WD=Q11451164|1=Asaka-shinnō}}; {{ill|Inoe-naishinnō|WD=Q7244649|1=Princess Inoe}}; {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955|1=Empress Kōken}}; {{ill|Motoi-ō|WD=Q11426431|1=Motoi-ō}}|native_name=不破内親王}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | parents = Father: {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}, Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}}, '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[:en:Empress Inoe|Empress Inoe]] and {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}}.
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, {{ill|764|en|764|WD=Q31163|lt=764|1=764}}, Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In {{ill|769|en|769|WD=Q30697|lt=769|1=769}}, Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside {{ill|Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|en|Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|WD=Q11582685|lt=Agata no Inukai no Anenoko|1=Agata no Inukai no Michiyo}}, [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to {{ill|Tosa Province|en|Tosa Province|WD=Q857330|lt=Tosa Province|1=Tosa Province}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December {{ill|772|en|772|WD=Q30729|lt=772|1=772}}, the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In {{ill|782|en|782|WD=Q30550|lt=782|1=782}}, her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to {{ill|Izu Province|en|Izu Province|WD=Q860928|lt=Izu Province|1=Izu Province}}. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to {{ill|Awaji Province|en|Awaji Province|WD=Q1053717|lt=Awaji Province|1=Awaji Province}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In {{ill|795|en|795|WD=Q30447|lt=795|1=795}}, she was moved to {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}}.<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted {{ill|leprosy|en|leprosy|WD=Q36956|lt=leprosy|1=Leprosy}} and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.

{{Infobox person|birth_date=0723|death_date=0795|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Shioyaki-ō|WD=Q11428805|1=Shioyaki-ō}}|children={{ill|Higami no Kawatsugu|WD=Q11549549|1=Higami no Kawatsugu}}; {{ill|Q11549551|WD=Q11549551|1=Hikami no Shikeshimaro}}|father={{ill|Shōmu|WD=Q349263|1=Emperor Shōmu}}|mother={{ill|Agatainukai no Hirotoji|WD=Q11582687|1=Agatainukai no Hirotoji}}|relatives={{ill|Asaka-shinnō|WD=Q11451164|1=Asaka-shinnō}}; {{ill|Inoe-naishinnō|WD=Q7244649|1=Princess Inoe}}; {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955|1=Empress Kōken}}; {{ill|Motoi-ō|WD=Q11426431|1=Motoi-ō}}|native_name=不破内親王}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Fuhwa Naishinnō (不破内親王) | image = | imagesize = | caption = | era = [[Nara Period]] - Early [[Heian Period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | renamed = Fuhwa (Naishinnō) → Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame → Fuhwa (Naishinnō) | aliases = | posthumous_name = | honorific_titles = | Buddhist_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | parents = Father: {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}, Mother: [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]] | siblings = [[Inoe Naishinnō]], {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}}, '''Fuhwa Naishinnō''', [[Prince Moto]], [[Prince Asaka]] | spouse = [[Prince Shioyaki]] (Hikami no Shioyaki) | children = [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]], several unnamed daughters | noteworthy = }}
'''Fuhwa Naishinnō''' (不破内親王) was an imperial princess of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}. Her mother was the consort [[Agata no Inukai no Hirotoji]]. She was the full sister of [[:en:Empress Inoe|Empress Inoe]] and {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}}.
== Biography == At an unknown date, Fuhwa Naishinnō married [[Prince Shioyaki]], a descendant of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. It is said that she was stripped of her title as imperial princess for a period, but the details are unclear. Historian Kōsuke Kawachi argues that this occurred during Emperor Shōmu's reign, suggesting strained relations between father and daughter. This tension might have contributed to the disparate treatment she received compared to her sister, [[Inoe Naishinnō]], and her brother-in-law, [[Prince Shirakabe]].<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōsuke Kawachi|title=Logic of the Imperial System in Ancient Political History|edition=Expanded Edition|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2014|pages=136-137}}</ref>
On September 18, {{ill|764|en|764|WD=Q31163|lt=764|1=764}}, Prince Shioyaki was killed for participating in the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 25, Emperor Junnin, Entry for September 29, Tenpyō-hōji 8</ref> However, Fuhwa Naishinnō and her son, [[Hikami no Shikeshimaro]], were spared punishment.
In {{ill|769|en|769|WD=Q30697|lt=769|1=769}}, Fuhwa Naishinnō, alongside {{ill|Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|en|Agata no Inukai no Michiyo|WD=Q11582685|lt=Agata no Inukai no Anenoko|1=Agata no Inukai no Michiyo}}, [[Oshisaka no Ōnakatsuhime|Princess Oshisaka]], and [[Princess Ishida]], was accused of plotting to curse [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]] to install her son, Hikami no Shikeshimaro, as emperor. She was stripped of her title, renamed '''Kuriyano Mahito no Kuriyame''', and banned from residing in the [[Heijō-kyō]] capital. Her son, Shikeshimaro, was exiled to {{ill|Tosa Province|en|Tosa Province|WD=Q857330|lt=Tosa Province|1=Tosa Province}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 29, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for May 25, Jingo-keiun 3</ref> In July of the same year, she was granted 40 households and 10 chō of rice paddies for subsistence.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 30, Empress Shōtoku, Entry for July 10, Jingo-keiun 3</ref>
In December {{ill|772|en|772|WD=Q30729|lt=772|1=772}}, the allegations were deemed false, and Fuhwa Naishinnō was restored to her imperial title.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 32, Emperor Kōnin, Entry for December 12, Hōki 3</ref>
In {{ill|782|en|782|WD=Q30550|lt=782|1=782}}, her son, [[Hikami no Kawatsugu]]{{Efn|Hikami no Shikeshimaro and Hikami no Kawatsugu may be the same person, according to Rikuo Hayashi.<ref>Rikuo Hayashi, "Political Conditions in the Early Kanmu Period," ''Kokugakuin University Hokkaido College Bulletin'', Volume 30, 2013, p.10, {{doi|10.24626/kokuteanc.30.0_1_1}}</ref>}}, was accused of rebellion and exiled to {{ill|Izu Province|en|Izu Province|WD=Q860928|lt=Izu Province|1=Izu Province}}. Fuhwa Naishinnō, her daughters, and others were exiled to {{ill|Awaji Province|en|Awaji Province|WD=Q1053717|lt=Awaji Province|1=Awaji Province}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Volume 37, Emperor Kanmu, Entries for Intercalary January 11 and 14, Ten'ō 2</ref> In {{ill|795|en|795|WD=Q30447|lt=795|1=795}}, she was moved to {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}}.<ref>''Nihon Kōki'', Volume 4, Emperor Kanmu, Entry for December 22, Enryaku 14 (Fragment)</ref> Her fate after this is unknown.
== Legend == [[File:松虫姫公園.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Statue of Matsumushi-hime and the cow said to have leaped into a pond]] A legend in [[Matsumushi-dera]] Temple, located in [[Inzai City]], {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, associates the site with Fuhwa Naishinnō.
The story tells of Princess Matsumushi (幼名 of Fuhwa Naishinnō) who contracted {{ill|leprosy|en|leprosy|WD=Q36956|lt=leprosy|1=Leprosy}} and was expelled from the imperial court.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Second Volume of the Sakura Archives|author=Sakura City Education Committee|date=1974|publisher=Sakura City Education Committee|pages=53}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Tone River Chronicles|author=Akama Matsudō, edited by Kunio Yanagita|date=1938|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|pages=209}}</ref> After praying to the Medicine Buddha, she was miraculously cured.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Her remains are enshrined at the mound behind the temple.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Some legends say Emperor Shōmu constructed the temple in gratitude for her recovery.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Digital Museum Map of Matsumushi-dera|url=https://www.chiba-muse.or.jp/NATURAL/special/hakuranzu/shohen/1059.html|publisher=Chiba Prefectural Museum|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
Another version, recorded in ''Tales of Bōsō'' (1983), claims the following: > Princess Matsumushi suffered from leprosy, with no cure in sight. In a dream, she was guided to a powerful Medicine Buddha enshrined in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}. Journeying eastward, she found a small temple in the Hagihara District and built a hermitage, praying earnestly for healing. Eventually, she recovered completely. Emperor Shōmu ordered {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|WD=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} to establish Matsumushi-dera Temple to commemorate her.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Matsumushi-dera Travel Guide|url=https://www.nihon-kankou.or.jp/|publisher=Japan National Tourism Organization|accessdate=2022-03-06}}</ref>
A statue representing her loyal cow, said to have leaped into a pond out of grief when left behind, is displayed in Matsumushi-hime Park in nearby Chiba New Town.
